data.title: Western Alliance Bank Shines On Margins, While Loan Chargeoffs May Cloud The Sky
data.description:
Intro: A Regional Bank In The American West, On A Roller-Coaster Ride Lately

With recent market volatility, today's article returns to a sector I covered often on this platform and that is regional banks, with the goal of finding dip-buying and undervalued opportunities on otherwise fundamentally solid businesses.

One of those banks, Phoenix-based Western Alliance Bancorp (NYSE:WAL), the parent of Western Alliance Bank, has seen its share price dive 13% over the last month.

Because of such a short-term roller coaster in markets, today's thesis is geared to investors focused on a longer-term horizon rather than day trading, and looking beyond the recent volatile day-to-day swings in bank stocks.

From its Seeking Alpha profile, we know this firm "provides various banking products and related services primarily in Arizona, California, and Nevada. It operates through Commercial and Consumer Related segments."

With its most recent dividend yield hovering above 2%, this stock is also one of my dividend ideas for the week, and my rating for this stock will be based on 9 factors I consider holistically to determine an average rating score, which includes a section on the effects of public policy, since we have seen how policy decisions can impact markets and stocks.

Thesis: An Undervalued Regional Bank With Expected Future Price Growth

My thesis today will present why I think this stock is a buying opportunity, and will consider 9 different factors, assigning a rating score to each.. with an average score determining whether it is a buy, sell, or hold.

In summary, as shown in the table below, I gave this stock an average rating score of "7" which justifies a buy rating.

Some key positive factors driving upside include: undervaluation and expected future price growth, the recent upgrade from Fitch, a dividend yield beating its sector, and a proven profit margin strength.

At the same time, some negatives to consider are that this stock did not have a great 10-year total return compared to the S&P500 index, it can be exposed to the rising trend of nonperforming loans and charge-offs, as well as interest rate risk.

The following article goes into each topic with a combination of third-party evidence, some historic data, and some future-minded guidance /outlooks too considering that FY25 Q1 results are due soon, so this article aims at previewing the firm to get ahead of the next earnings call.

Company's Positive Outlook For 2025 Could Depend On More Loan Growth Like In 2024

When it comes to the business of regional banks like this one, I think what could drive future revenue growth is recent growth in loans as that drives future interest income, but also business acquisitions of other banks and its customer base along with new branch openings. Consider, for example, that loans are usually something a customer might pay years of interest on.

Notable from this firm's FY24 Q4 presentation from late January, Q4 saw YoY loan growth of 6.7%, which I believe is a solid upside factor as it drives interest income:

From my research, it seems this bank essentially makes most of its revenue from interest, both on loans and on its securities portfolio, and makes some revenue from fees, but does not really have any major fee-driven business like asset management or wealth advisory, as big banks like Morgan Stanley (MS) may have.

Because of this, future revenue can be impacted a lot more heavily by interest rate yields, and the firm did mention the effect of lower yields in their Q4 presentation.

For example, the following chart shows a slight YoY drop in yield on their securities portfolio, however it was nothing too drastic:

Hence, a macro factor that could impact this bank looking ahead is loan demand.

The company did not mention any business acquisitions recently, or new branch growth, that I could find, however the firm did add "an aerospace and defense banker team, and a food and agriculture banker team, both based in California," according to a fall 2024 article in industry site Banking Dive, as the bank attempts greater penetration of those business segments, which could drive future growth I think, setting this regional bank up as a specialist in specific business banking segments.

As the above factors could be positive drivers, in terms of revenue growth the company's own outlook for FY25 called for +6-8% growth in both interest and non-interest income, which I think could attract bullish investors putting more weight on growth, thereby adding support to the share price.

Earnings Growth Can Be Driven By Continued Solid Profit Margin Within Sector

When getting a sense for this bank's earnings growth potential, a metric I like to consider is the net income margin, or profit margin, to get an idea for the firm's efficiency at turning a profit vs similar peers in the same sector.

From Seeking Alpha data, we can see that the firm achieved a margin of 26%, about 16% better than its sector average.

To get more granular, I picked 4 regional banks to compare to, in different regions of the US (Texas, Southeast, Mid-Atlantic, Northeast) and they are: Cullen-Frost (CFR), Farmers National (FMNB), Regions (RF), and Valley National (VLY). We can see from the table below that Western Alliance Bank has held up well among this peer group of regionals, and whose margin is only around 5% lower than the group average.

Another metric to point out is from the firm's Q4 presentation, and that is the efficiency ratio, since non-interest expenses like operating costs are a major component that can impact the bottom line and future earnings.

Although the company beat its sector in profit margin, a challenge I think it will face is its efficiency ratio has seen a YoY decline as of Q4, as non-interest expenses creeped higher:

So, what I think investors should look for in the upcoming Q1 earnings results is whether this negative trend in the efficiency ratio has continued, which could indicate the company will need to take more steps to control costs.

A strong margin can contribute to solid net income results and EPS, and notable to mention is that this firm's EPS growth (GAAP) both on a YoY and fwd basis has exceeded its sector average. For example, its fwd EPS growth is +17% while the sector average is just under 10%.

This is relevant because the next EPS will be a key headline metric that both investors and analysts will react to, and I am weighing more towards it being a positive reaction. Consider that it was reported on Apr. 10th by Yahoo! Finance and Zack's Market Research that "the market expects Western Alliance to deliver a year-over-year increase in earnings on higher revenues when it reports results for the quarter ended March 2025."

Public Policy Is Driving Continually Changing Sentiment, But Not Indication To Panic

In light of recent market events, I added a new section going forward and here I will briefly talk about the effects of public policy on this firm and sector.

For instance, a Reuters article on Apr. 3rd pointed out that if tariff policy leads to a recessionary environment, an effect could be that "it will impact loan growth and credit quality," however also pointing out that "the U.S. banking sector has broadly been performing well with robust earnings and non-performing loans under check, prompting some analysts to predict the sector may yet weather the challenges without a significant hit to earnings."

So, I don't believe headline-inducing tariff policy by itself will necessarily make or break this sector, since tariffs were not invented yesterday, and it is more about whether such tariffs would trigger a widespread US recession and drop in loan demand, but also ability for businesses to pay their debts if a recession leads to lower incomes.

As for interest rates, that is more in the realm of the Federal Reserve which is not a federal cabinet agency but still deserves mention within public policy.

I would note that Fed rate tracker CME Fedwatch (as of Sunday evening data) now indicates an 60% probability the Fed keeps its target interest rate the same after its May 7th meeting, while last week it had been closer to 83%, so it appears the probability of a rate cut has increased. For June's Fed meeting, it predicts a nearly 50% chance of a rate cut.

My opinion is that the rate cut may be expected as a way to stave off a recession. If this translates into lower interest rates across the board, I expect a boost in loan demand as debt gets cheaper.

Cheaper debt may not necessarily be a huge demand driver for bank loans if there is a large enough recession, though, as businesses and consumers may both cut back on borrowing.

A key headline that impacted markets was the recession outlook from a major bank, as reported in Investopedia on Apr. 7th, and other media too, when Goldman Sachs had cut their GDP growth forecast for 2025, citing tariff risk.

Interestingly, just days after that and by the middle of last week they reversed course. As reported by the Wall St Journal on Apr. 9th:

We are reverting to our previous non-recession baseline forecast with GDP growth of 0.5% and a 45% probability of recession.

Complementary to this new update, according to a news article by Seeking Alpha last week, NYC-based Fundstrat Advisors said the following:

Recession risks remain overblown due to significant offsets that cushion the tariff blow.. From a tactical perspective, markets are deeply oversold and ripe for a reversal.

It is also worth mentioning that another major financial firm, Wells Fargo, said last Tuesday that " they're seeing a handful of promising signs that the US economy may be able to avoid a downturn this year," according to Business Insider, as two positive factors were rising incomes and household wealth.

So, I don't think there is an overly strong case in support of a severe recession happening, at least not one that would dampen loan demand, particularly if Fed rate cuts boosts demand for cheap debt.

Balance Sheet Risk Improved With Fitch Upgrade, Though Risks Remain

When it comes to balance sheet risk, for this type of business what comes to mind is how much potential loan loss its loan book is exposed to, what kind of real estate exposure it has, and also its corporate debt and bond holdings.

One bright spot to mention is that this bank has lowered its debt/equity over the last 5 years down to 0.96, indicating lower corporate debt in relation to equity, which I think is a positive factor.

The trend I am more cautious about is the increasing trend we see in non-performing loans, and the Q4 presentation clearly shows a YoY uptick (though a small one) in that trend, although NPLs still remain less than 0.70% of loans:

Paired with that, the same results (figures in millions$$) point to an upward trend of loan charge-offs comparing on a YoY basis, which could indicate clients having increasing difficulty paying back debts.

Consider a March article by Marketplace.org, which pointed to a larger issue and it is primarily consumer credit card debt:

In 2024, Americans defaulted on $59 billion in credit card debt — a 34% jump from 2023.. inflation is a huge factor.

This bank also made an effort to mention its exposure to commercial real estate, which I think is a good thing as investor will want to know about that.

From its Q4 presentation, it appears that 18% of its total loan book is tied to commercial real estate, however that CRE exposure is diversified across multiple sub-segments, with 24% exposed to office property. So, in the larger picture, exposure to office real estate in relation to the overall size of the loan portfolio is not relatively huge.

I should also mention that major agency Fitch reported in February 2025 that it upgraded this bank to a "BBB" which is an investment-grade rating, saying one key factor behind the upgrade was "increased capital levels and improved ratios, alongside a reduction in the company's loan-to-deposit (LTD) ratio."

Finally, its fixed-income securities portfolio is certainly subject to interest rate risk, and we already mentioned the likelihood of Fed rate cuts soon, but recently we also saw Treasury yields jump after a bond selloff, so more bond selloffs could impact asset values for firms with a large bond portfolio but also boost yields which could lead to more interest income from higher-yielding bonds.

A Lagging Factor is The 10 Year Return vs S&P500 Index

Particularly since short-term returns on stocks, as we have seen over the last few weeks, can swing wildly, in this section I wanted to get a closer look at what kind of total return (which includes dividend income as well as price return) this stock has achieved over the last 10 year period, compared to a common index being tracked like the S&P500 index.

Using momentum data from Seeking Alpha, I created the following table that compares WAL vs the index, showing its total return has underperformed the index by nearly 30%.

While this may reflect historical performance and not future, I am including it to show whether this stock has proven itself over a longer term in either being close to or beating a major index, which it has not really done well so far.

Many stocks are impacted by recent price dives, but my question is how well some of them recover and achieve a total return for its investors in the longer run, and I think a good benchmark is being +/- 10% vs the S&P 500, or far outperforming it.

Hence, this stock got a lower score in this category for this reason, pulling down its overall average rating score a bit.

Dividend Yield Lags Behind Several Regional Banks

When it comes to another dividend component, yield, I would not put this stock in a high-yield category, with a yield of just 2.4% as of this article writing, while the sector average is closer to 3.5%.

Among the regional banking comparison group I created earlier, here is a comparison of their dividend yields, placing this bank last among the group, so if I was a new buyer and building a high-yield portfolio was my primary focus it seems a bank like New-Jersey based Valley National with its +5% yield is more suitable for that type of goal:

This above-average yield gave this stock a little boost in its average rating score, and could particularly be of interest to yield-driven investors.

A Proven Return on Equity Among Peers

Another factor I have added, starting with this article, is whether a firm has a proven model to achieve strong return on equity, either meeting or exceeding its sector and or peer group, as a benchmark to compare against.

In particular this is an interesting data point considering the vast amount of assets this bank holds.

We can see that this bank's return on common equity was 12.71%, well above the sector average of 10.4%, which is already impressive.

Also, when I compared against the peer group mentioned earlier, this bank stands up strong being 2nd place within this group pf 5 regionals:

Although this data may be based on historic results and not future projections, I think it lays a strong foundation for this firm to present an attractive case for investors who put more emphasis on having a proven model for achieving a strong return on shareholder equity.

Share Price & Valuation Took A Major Dip Below The Average, Creating Potential Opportunity

In this section, we will take a look at both the share price and a few valuation metrics.

Putting aside the short-term volatility of the last week, I created this chart to show this stock's price movement over 5 years vs its 200-day SMA and 5 year high/low:

Although the stock got a boost, along with many others, on Wednesday after a pause on tariffs for many countries was announced, the stock is still over 20% below its 200-day SMA, going into Monday. It also shows that this stock can be impacted greatly in the short-term by sudden public policy announcements.

Further, Seeking Alpha's valuation data shows metrics like the fwd P/E (GAAP) down to 7.27, well below the sector average, while the trailing P/B is around 1.07 and almost in line with the sector average.

This appears like value-driven investors on a shopping spree this week may take a closer look at this company, considering such low valuations and price dip.

My own price target for this stock is $62.96 for Dec. 2025, $75.46 for Dec. 2026, and $82.22 for Dec. 2027, so based on my scenario a longer-term investor holding this stock at least a few years could see a +30% gain by Dec. 2027 if this projection is realized:

The guidance is based on the fwd P/E multiple right now multiplied by the analyst consensus estimate on EPS for those periods. This is also mentioned as a common price target methodology in sites like Public.com, where the Price Target = Projected EPS × Expected P/E Ratio.

From my guidance above, a shorter-term investor might not benefit as much as a longer-term one.

Additional Sector Risks: Market Competition And Cyber Incidents

I added this section to include any sector-driven risk factors that have not already been mentioned, and give those factors either an upside or downside score, depending on what I think its impact would be to this bank.

One risk factor to consider is stiff market competition within banking, as with any sector, and what kind of market leadership position this firm has as a "brand".

From Federal Reserve data, we can see that this bank is merely ranked #33 among US commercial banks, placing it well behind large names who may also be doing business in those western regions of the US this bank calls home.

Other data, from the Phoenix Business Journal , says although Western Alliance has the largest deposit base locally in Phoenix, it is being chased right behind by JP Morgan Chase (JPM), who is also the largest bank in the US, and also is competing closely for deposits in the entire state.

So, investors should consider that even though this may be an important regional player and a major bank in Phoenix and Arizona, the large national banking names are increasingly at its heels and trying to capture that regional market share, but also have the vast scale and resources to do so.

Cyber risks can be headline-inducing for this sector, and can hurt brand reputation, as we have seen with the March 21st article in Security Magazine who reported that 22,000 customers were impacted by a data breach at Western Alliance Bank. In fact, it would be almost irresponsible for me as an analyst not to have mentioned this incident while covering this company.

The "flaw was found in a third-party software, and it was used to hack a select few Western Alliance systems to exfiltrate files," according to the article.

This type of incident, I think, underscores how vulnerable banks are, much like insurance firms, as they hold vast amounts of sensitive client data which can be stolen.

For the above two reasons, stiff market competition and potential for large-scale cyber attacks that impact business and brand reputation, I gave this firm a slightly lower score in the category of "sector risk factors," which lowers it overall from potentially being a strong buy.

Conclusion: Buy The Undervaluation

In summary, my research has determined this stock is a buy.

Here is the rating table I used, with my weighted scores, and the key used:

To summarize today's thesis, this stock just passed into the buy category from potentially being only a hold, as several categories weighed more towards the buy side this time, and I showed why in the previous sections.

I gave the largest buy-side weight to the current valuation and 3-year price targets, which I determined to be favorable for longer-term investors. In addition, the public policy risks of recesssion-driving tariffs and interest rate decisions by the Fed did not present enough downside this time around, from evidence I found.

data.title: SoFi Technologies' Growth Engine Just Shifted Gears
data.description:
SoFi Technologies (NASDAQ:SOFI) is no longer the niche digital lender it was in the past. Over the last few years, it has grown increasingly to be a full-stack, vertically integrated financial platform offering lending, banking, investments, credit cards, and infrastructure technology under one digital umbrella.

SoFi is evolving from a lending-first disruptor into a diversified fintech platform with powerful earnings potential. In 2024, fee-based segments made up nearly half of revenue, signaling a major pivot toward capital-light, high-ROE growth. With expanding product monetization and strategic execution, SoFi is building a durable engine for long-term compounding.

From Lending Giant to Fintech Flywheel

Its 2024 performance was one of the pinnacle moments in this journey. It recorded historic revenue, reported five consecutive GAAP profitable quarters, and brought fee-based revenue almost to half of the total adjusted net revenue. SoFi's adjusted net revenue in 2024 was $2.61 billion, 26% greater than last year. More indicative of the strength at hand, though, is the acceleration in non-lending revenue.

Its Technology Platform and Financial Services businesses recorded $1.2 billion in revenue, or 54% year-over-year, and now represent 47% of total revenue, versus 38% in 2023. It's diversification that graphically describes the intentional execution of a strategy to reduce the company's reliance on net interest income and establish a higher-ROE, capital-light business.

Loans, SoFi's largest category, accounted for $1.45 billion of adjusted revenue, driven by $23.2 billion in mortgage, student, and personal loan originations. Its in-house banking subsidiary, fueled by $25 billion of member deposits, has driven funding cost reductions and balance sheet management that other fintech can't.

Lending as the Foundation: Scaling Through Optionality and Efficiency

In Lending, profitability and growth are driven by personal loans. These loans are resilient in high interest environments, while both home lending and student loan refinancing are interest rate-sensitive and cyclic. SoFi's balance sheet approach is rooted in optionality: it keeps a proportion of the loans to earn interest income, sells a proportion through whole-loan channels, and increasingly originates directly to institutions through LPB.

SoFi sold $5.8 billion of whole loans in 2024, securitized $1.2 billion, and sourced $2.1 billion through LPB to partners such as Fortress. Its hybrid model maximizes capital productivity without compromising member acquisition while de-risking the balance sheet. Significantly, the LPB model enables SoFi to keep servicing rights and member relationships and presents downstream cross-sell opportunities even if loans are off its books.

The Financial Services division, which features SoFi Invest, SoFi Money, credit cards, and newer offerings such as Relay and SoFi at Work, achieved profitability in its inaugural year. It is a significant achievement in light of its capital-light status. Most notably, new credit card launches and the robo-advisor platform drove greater engagement and increased monetization potential. Brokerage, interchange, and advisory referral fees drove most of the revenue in this division. As these products mature and value per user for life rises,

Financial Services can potentially equal Lending in terms of profit contribution. Its profitability in early stages remains delicate, however, and consistent gains will rely on minimizing the cost of customer acquisition and rising average revenue per user through high-frequency use.

Galileo and Technisys are the core of SoFi's vision to serve as an infrastructure platform for the fintech and banking world. In 2024, Galileo processed over one billion transactions and introduced its U.S. core banking product. Those victories, together with the Direct Express deal, set the Technology Platform on course for recurring revenue in the longer term. Contribution margins are, however, modest, given the pressure on prices and cost of integration. Future potential depends on layering in additional products, fraud protection, compliance APIs, and identity solutions that yield better margins. Expansion internationally, especially in Latin America, may also spur growth if managed correctly, though there is growing competition in banking-as-a-service.

SoFi’s Next Test: Can It Outrun Its Own Success?

Ahead of SoFi's next earnings, the market is anticipating a slowdown, or at least on paper. Following a record Q4 2024 in which the company beat per-share earnings expectations, the full-year 2025 consensus earnings now stand at $0.26 per share, implying a 33% drop YoY. However, the earnings decline is somewhat misleading, as last year’s results were boosted by a one-time deferred tax benefit.

The quarterly pace is still intact. SoFi beat Q1, Q2, and Q3 2024 estimates by a penny each quarter, leading to the enormous Q4 beat. SoFi is expected to see steady EPS growth in Q1, Q2, and Q3 of FY2025, followed by a significant decline in Q4 due to the comp last year. Revenue estimates remain at $3.17 billion in 2025.

Investors also need to listen intently to management discussions of cross-sell traction, Lending margin strength, and additional monetization of the LPB and Galileo platforms. If SoFi can keep growing fee-based revenue, drive CAC down, and show better unit economics, it may well beat as well as meet these lofty expectations.

If, however, margin squeeze or integration issues arise, the stock will be vulnerable, given its premium valuation. Having set a high bar and visibility lengthening, this next quarter's report may determine if 2024 was a break-out year or only the start.

Valuation Aligned to Implementation: A Premium with Performance Requirements

SoFi's valuation today puts it in the higher-multiple group of names in the fintech and financial services group. As of early 2025, it trades for roughly 42x forward earnings on the consensus 2025 EPS estimate of $0.26. This is comparable to 21x on 2026 expectations, which would involve nearly doubling earnings to $0.50. Based on the estimates, SoFi is trading on about 3.6x forward price/sales in 2025 and 3.04x in 2026. Those figures signal that the market is imbuing high confidence in the company's capacity to maintain its growth rate, expand margins, and expand monetization throughout the ecosystem.

Relative to traditional banking or even certain digital-native peers, SoFi's valuation appears aggressive on the surface. To cite a couple of examples, Upstart (UPST) and LendingClub (LC) are valued on notably lower revenue and EBITDA multiples, driven by their exposure to cyclical lending and the absence of platform diversification. Block (XYZ), even though it's got a larger ecosystem in which to operate, is valued on a lower revenue multiple while also boasting better historic margins.

However, SoFi's premium is not simply a reflection of growth, it's a wager on strategic execution. What investors are really baking in is the continuation of SoFi's successful transition to fee-based revenue, disciplined operating leverage, and capacity to realize value on its expanding user base through a high-retention, multi-product strategy.

But this premium valuation comes with a tight margin of error. An LPB volume stumble, worse-than-anticipated adoption of financial services products, or teething issues in integrating Galileo and Technisys may trigger a derating. Investors will need to compare the potential upsides of SoFi becoming a financial super-app and enterprise platform versus the risk of macro headwinds or operational misfires that might impede its compounding flywheel.

For long-term investors, the current multiple may be fair if the company can maintain 25–30% adjusted EBITDA margins and grow EPS by a 40–50% CAGR over the next three years. However, in the near term, modest underperformance versus expectations may trigger volatility.

A Fintech Compounding Machine in the Making, But Not Without Risks

The investment thesis in SoFi is strong, particularly for investors looking for exposure to the long-term trend toward digital-first finance. Its vertical integration, well-established brand among younger customers, and diversifying model of revenue provide it with a platform that few other fintechs enjoy.

Its flywheel is gaining traction as members purchase additional products, financial service monetization increases, and the lending business becomes profitable and well-leveraged. In contrast to its neobank peers, which rely on interchange or high-yield savings tricks, SoFi enjoys a layered ecosystem, both consumer-facing and enterprise, that is starting to exhibit operating leverage.

Nevertheless, there are real risks. Regulatory attention is likely to increase, notably in terms of banking charters and LPB businesses. An extended environment of high rates may depress origination demand, notably in rate-sensitive products like mortgages. LPB scaling-up is encouraging, although it adds vulnerability to partners such as Fortress. Although there are longer-term benefits in Galileo and Technisys, execution risk exists, notably in scaling B2B infrastructure profitably.

SoFi's valuation today is predicated on faith in the narrative, and it also builds in very little margin for error. But for those willing to risk near-term volatility and watch important measurements such as contribution margin, product adoption speed, and cross-sell penetration, SoFi presents the potential to invest in one of the few fintechs that would end up being a structural compounder.

The journey from fintech disruptor to market leader is never linear. But in SoFi's situation, the pieces are falling in place, and if management keeps executing, the reward can be significant.

Bottom Line

SoFi's growth narrative is true, yet maintaining expanding margins and cross-segment execution will be the challenge test. For investors, the risk-reward is predicated upon its capability of converting innovation to consistent, diversified cash flow rather than simple user growth.

Rose’s Income Garden “RIG” March Raises

Rose's Income Garden portfolio is diversified into 11+ sectors, the + represents preferred shares and some ETFs. “RIG” ended 2024 with a dividend yield of 6.18% which has risen to 6.5% with the recent market action. The total portfolio is completely tracked on a trading daily basis at The Macro Trading Factory service.

“RIG” has 84 companies and all of them pay dividends. The months of March, June, September and December are the most popular months for quarterly paying companies including “RIG”.

Total March income was up 5.1% from 2024 from 42 company payments of which 10 are monthly, 30 quarterly and 2 were special only.

8 Raises were the highlight from the 42 company payers and this is a review of those.

Some of the technical information such as 5-yr DGR/ Dividend growth rate comes from FAST Graphs, a paid subscriber service I use.

8 Raises

1 - WEC Energy Group

Regulated electric utility headquartered in Milwaukee, WI. since 1896 with an “A-” S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The 6.9% dividend raise of 5.75c from 83.5c = 89.25c gives it a 22-year record of raising it.

The yearly dividend of $3.57 gives it a 3.34% yield at a price of $106.96, and it has a payout of 68.3%.

The 5-year DGR = 7.19%, so the recent raise of 6.9% was not quite as high as the average but darn close.

Earnings future estimates from "FG" indicate 7% for the next 3 years into 2027. The dividend estimates also look to rise along with those earnings.

Chowder #

The chowder # is obtained by adding the 5-yr dividend growth rate with the current dividend yield. It is a measure of investing success and yield for the future and is only meant to be used for a purchase.

C # = 10.53 (3.34 +7.19) is higher than the desired # of 8 for a utility, so it passes that test.

Rose Take and Recommendation

This is a quality dividend payer with nice dividend growth, but I do like it with a yield of 3.9 -4% for a purchase. The chart above shows the price can drop lower, and I will watch for myself for that reason alone. It is in my portfolio and is a core holding.

2 - Pfizer

Healthcare Big Pharma company headquartered in NY. since 1849 with an “A” S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The dividend raise of 1c from 42c to 43c = 2.4% and allows it to keep a now 15-year record intact.

The yearly dividend $1.72 gives it a yield = 7.85% at a price of $21.91.

Payout is down to 54% from 89% for 2023, so the dividend most likely is very safe.

5-year DGR = 4.28% makes the recent raise a disappointment. “FG” indicates that to be a trend, with only 1c raises into 2027.

Earnings also look to be disappointing and look to continue at or near $3 or so through 2027 using “FG” estimates.

Chowder # = 11.1 (2.4 +7.85) is higher than 8, which is desired for high yield “HY” stocks. It only passes secondary for being HY and definitely not for being a good dividend raiser.

Rose Take and Recommendation

The flat earnings and dividends are very much reflected in the price, and it might just stay that way for some time. If you want an income stock with a 7.85% yield, this is exactly what Pfizer is right now, so it's a buy for that reason alone.

3 - Chevron

Energy IOC/ Integrated Oil & Gas Company headquartered in CA. since 1879 with an “AA-” S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The dividend raise of 8c from $1.63 to $1.71 = 4.9% giving it a 38-year record of raising it.

The yearly dividend of $6.84 gives it a yield of 5.04% at a price of $135.63 with a payout of 64.88%.

5-year DGR = 6.51%, so the recent raise was not as high as the average.

Earnings are down in 2025 and most likely the reason for the price drop, but “FG” estimates them to rise nicely by double digits into 2026 and 2027.

Chowder # = 11.55 (5.04 +6.51) is higher than the desired # of 8 for an energy stock so it passes that test.

Rose Take and Recommendation

The recent price drop is attractive along with the very nice high yield for this safe, reliable company. I own it and watch for perhaps just a little lower price as all the tariff and “drill, baby drill” controversy are still fresh and mostly undecided, but the yield is calling to buy for many that might not own any.

4 - McDonald's

Consumer cyclical restaurant headquartered in Chicago, IL. since 1940 with a BBB+ S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The dividend raise of 8c from $1.67 to $1.77= 6% giving it a 49-year record of raising it.

Yearly dividend of $7.08 gives it a yield of 2.28% at a price of $309.90 with a payout of 57.85%.

5-year DGR = 7.5%, so the recent raise was nice but not quite up to average.

Earnings are moving along nicely and improving from a down year in 2024 but “FG” shows mid to high single digit % increases along with the dividend increases staying in the 5-6% range.

Chowder # = 9.78 (2.28 +7.5) is not quite near the desired # of 15 for a low yield stock as this is now. If it were over 2.5% it would need a 12, so it is not close to that either. The price would need to go lower to satisfy getting a higher yield and a lower C#.

Rose Take and Recommendation

Safe, reliable dividend and company that most likely is sitting just a bit over fair value. I would watch again for a price dip to buy, as it did hit near $250 last year which could happen again, maybe. It is a hold for "RIG".

5 - Duke Energy

Electric and Gas utility headquartered in Charlotte, NC. since 1904 with a BBB+ S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The dividend raise of .005c from $1.045 to $1.05 = 0.5% giving it a 20-year record of raising it.

The yearly dividend of $4.20 gives it 3.53% yield at a price of $118.94 with a payout of 70%.

5-year DGR = 2.03%, so the recent raise was extremely annoying and surprising.

Earnings are rising by mid-single digits using “FG” so the low dividend raise was unexpected. Management might have something planned to use the cash elsewhere.

Chowder # = 5.56 (2.03 +3.53) which is nowhere near the 8 needed to pass for a utility.

Rose Take and Recommendation

The price looks to be somewhat overvalued especially with the low yield and now a poor dividend raise does not make it a buy at all. It might even get a trim if the price goes higher. I am watching it closely, but it does have reliability with a safe dividend, which makes it at least a hold.

6 - Shell plc

Energy IOC/ Integrated Oil & Gas Company headquartered in London, UK. since 1907 with an “A+” S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The dividend raise of 2.8c from 68.8c to 71.6c = 4.1% giving it a 2-year record of raising it.

The yearly dividend of $2.86 gives it a yield of 4.61% at a price of $62.09 with a payout of 36.6%.

5-year DGR = 2.97%, so the recent raise was extremely welcome and appreciated, but it still is recovering from having cut it a few years ago.

Earnings were down in 2024 by double digits and look to fall by 5% in 2025 and rise by mid-single digits into 2026 and 2027 using “FG” estimates.

Chowder # = 7.58 (2.97 +4.61) which is almost near the 8 needed to pass for an energy stock, but sadly, it does not pass using that metric.

Rose Take and Recommendation

The recent price drop does make it more attractive, but I do see perhaps even a lower price coming which makes it a hold for “RIG” and 5% yield would get my attention for sure.

7 - Home Depot

Retail home improvement consumer cyclical headquartered in Atlanta, GA and incorporated in 1978 has an “A” S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The dividend raise of 5c from $2.25 to $2.30= 2.2%, giving it a 15-year record of raising it.

Yearly dividend of $9.20 gives it a yield of 2.6% at a price of $353.86 with a payout of 59.1%.

5-year DGR = 10.6%, so the recent raise was completely way low and unappreciated.

The earnings that were down in 2024 by 9%, look to rise 1% in 2025 but fall by 1% in 2026, so that is what I call flat and most likely the reason for a flat low and lousy dividend raise. Earnings estimates do look up double digits or 10% for 2027 and 2028 while the dividend looks to rise better but not into double digits.

Chowder # = 13.2 (10.6 +2.6) which is above the 12 needed to pass for a stock with a yield over 2.5%.

Rose Take and Recommendation

It is a quality safe dividend, but the low raise makes the C# metric not appropriate, and I would wait on an even lower price to make it a buy.

8 - Broadcom

Technology semiconductor company headquartered in Palo Alto, CA. has a BBB- S&P credit rating.

5 year Seeking Alpha Price and technical chart

The dividend raise of 6c from 53c to 59c= 11.3% giving it a 15-year record of raising it.

The yearly dividend of $2.36 gives it a yield of 1.3% at a price of $181.94 with a payout of 43.22%.

5-year DGR = 14.78%, so the raise was wonderful but still a bit under expectations.

Using “FG” earnings look to rise by 36% for 2025, but they are down to mid double digits of 18% for 2026 and 16% for 2027. The current P/E even with the price decline is ~32, which goes good with 36% earnings but not with lower future estimates as mentioned.

Chowder # = 16.08 (14.78 +1.3) is above the 15 needed to pass for a growth stock with a yield under 2.5%.

Rose Take and Recommendation

Quality tech stock with low dividend payout but probably getting better valued as the price drops. I consider it a hold and watch for earnings reports to show higher guidance to own more.

Summary/ Conclusion

“RIG” is constructed as an income portfolio and all the stocks have dividends, making it important to have them give raises. All of the 8 stocks that gave raises are staying in "RIG" but will constantly be under review for growing future dividends. The income is on the rise by 5.1% from 2024. The current forward yield is 6.44% with a portfolio value that has followed the recent market declines by being down 1.45% YTD but still beats SPY by 7.49% which is down 8.9% YTD.

Happy Investing to All.

data.title: Market Outlook: What Will Happen Next Week
data.description:
Introduction

Last week, the markets again were very volatile. While we saw many deep red days, we also saw the second largest intraday range ever in the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) and Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ). In this post, I will give you an overview of what might happen during next week.

Data Releases

Next week, there won't be that many very important US data releases. On Wednesday, US Retail Sales will be published. While they increased by 0.2% the last time, the market is now pricing in a gigantic 1.3% increase, which would be the highest in 1 year. As a result, a regression analysis (shown in Figure 1) yields a far lower expected increase of around 0.3%. This means either that there should have happened a fundamental shift in the market or that the market is overestimating the potential growth rate of retail sales.

Another important US data release is on Building Permits and Housing Starts on Friday. Building Permits are expected to be at 1.46M, slightly up from the latest reported number of 1.459M. Additionally, housing starts are expected to be lower than the last time, at 1.41M instead of 1.501M. The regression in Figure 2 shows the data points for housing starts. While it agrees with the expectations of lower starts, it estimates them to be even lower at 1.33M. Nevertheless, this should only be seen as an estimation of the trend and not as a reliable forecasting technique.

Apart from US data, there is much to unpack. On the night from Tuesday to Wednesday, China will report its GDP growth rate. The market currently shows a consensus of 5.1%, down from 5.4%. Additionally, we will see the BOC interest rate decision on Wednesday, while the ECB interest rate decision follows on Thursday. The BOC is expected not to cut interest rates, the market prices in a 25bp rate cut from the ECB. We will end the week with Japan reporting inflation data on Friday.

In terms of earnings, we will see The Goldman Sachs Group (GS) report earnings on Monday at a consensus EPS of $12.71. On Tuesday, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Bank of America Corporation (BAC), and Citigroup Inc. (C) will follow at estimated EPS of $2.57, $0.81, and $1.84, respectively. The last big earnings release will be by Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company on Thursday. Here, the market prices in an EPS of $2.02.

Outlook

Figure 3 shows the VIX. As can be seen, the index is currently at 37.55, which still signals extreme volatility. Since volatility is a great predictor of volatility and since none of the fundamental problems driving stock market volatility have been resolved, we can expect another extremely volatile week. The focus of investors will again be tariffs, with even more potential in the US-China trade war being the main concern. Apart from that, investors will likely focus on possible trade deals that will be made or that fail. As Trump said last week, if negotiations fail, the 90-day delay on tariffs could be taken back for some countries. Even if this only happens to one country, the news could be detrimental for the market. Additionally, Trump could even want to fail negotiations to show other countries that they have to give him even more than they currently want to. Nevertheless, in the night from Friday to Saturday, an exemption of US import tariffs for phones, computers, and other electronics was released. This will likely cause the markets to rise on Monday, with QQQ benefiting the most as it is quite tech-heavy. Nevertheless, the news should not be taken as being that bullish, since Trump got nothing in return and put himself in a worse position to negotiate tariffs against China back to where they were a few weeks ago. Because of this, I remain rather bearish, although the exemption is a step in the right direction.

In bonds such as the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT), we will likely see another week of uncertainty. In my opinion, prices are likely to continue falling as long as there is no clear credit event happening that signals to the market that the Fed will cut. While there are hopes for a rate cut, the more likely scenario will be for the Fed to wait and see how tariffs will affect inflation. I will talk about this more in a few weeks when the Fed meeting is coming up. Concerning the potential credit event, Figure 4 shows the moving averages of the weekly bankruptcy filings. As can be seen, the 4-week moving average currently shows an upward spike that was last seen during 2008 and 2020. This makes shorting treasuries very risky at the moment.

Conclusion

Since the upcoming week will not have extremely important data releases in the US markets, the focus will likely lie on tariffs again, especially considering Trump's recent announcement of tariff exemptions for smartphones. While a rally on Monday can be expected, the short- to medium-term outlook remains rather bearish. Nevertheless, I will upgrade QQQ from a “strong sell” to only a “sell”, while SPY remains at “sell”. Concerning treasuries, I revise my recent “strong sell” rating to a “hold” rating as the tail risk of a credit event balances out the expected return of continuing to short them.

data.title: Alphabet May Become The Largest Company In The World, Here's How
data.description:
Alphabet May Become the Largest Company in the World or get mighty close, Here’s How

Tough market

This week has not been easy for the market participant, whether a trader, medium-term speculator, or buy-and-hold investor. Even the pros are having a hard time as hedge funds have reported losses on either side of the trade, long or short. This is a yo-yo market where stocks shoot up 12% one day, then down big the next, then up big again. What’s the little guy to do? Since the 3rd week of February, I have been moving away from stressing trading, where now I just don’t. When this VIX is this high, the volatility is enormous, trading is really dangerous. Once the VIX comes back down to 17-16-15, I am avoiding it. What I have been doing is concentrating on my investment account, and concentrating on the best quality large capitalization stocks as they have been coming down. For the last several years, I had relatively small positions in the Mag7 names, and looked for medium-sized stocks that were dislocated from their valuation, and traded at a discount that I could gain from. With big-cap tech selling at a discount of as much as 30%, concentrating on growing positions here made sense.

Some in the financial media said, Mag 7 stocks were approaching bubble territory at their highs. I don’t think so

Some might counter this logic by saying that the old highs were unrealistic, and should not be used as an attainable target. It has been my experience that at some point, sometimes very soon, large-cap growth, indeed all sizes growth stocks of quality, usually regain their old highs.

Furthermore, this sharp comedown is due to a lack of visibility, when that happens, only the worst-case scenarios are entertained. Growth stocks had a higher valuation because inflation was lower; that is not a bubble. We’ve lived through bubbles, and this is not that. Now there is fear that prices will go higher and that will reignite inflation, but we are not seeing that right now. For me, these are investments, so if it takes to the end of the year, or 12 months from now, a 30% is not shabby at all, even 20% for that matter. So I remain confident that this is the right strategy for me. Once the policy chaos with tariffs calms down, I will widen my target area to include smaller stocks again. I have been investing in a very few smaller-sized stocks, with the vast bulk again being the largest well-known names.

I know that many opinion writers and CIOs of investment organizations are clamoring for earnings estimates to be cut, and that stock valuations are still too high, that they should be at 14 to 15 times. With those calculations, we should trade at 3800 to 4000 on the S&P 500. We are also admonished to ignore the current quarter's earnings since most of it was before this “reciprocal tariffs”. So far, the sample of companies reporting is small, but for the most part, have not disappointed. I believe that this quarter's earnings are just as valid as any, and there was already talk of recession/stagflation in Q1. I am not sure how the financial media is still flogging stagflation when the CPI and PPI for March came in so benignly. So, no, Stagflation is not on the menu, nor is recession; the tariff brouhaha could cause a slowdown. This is because the CEOs of corporations of all sizes have halted investments and commitments until there is more clarity. We haven’t seen any slowdown in employment, and that is the key indicator for recession. Maybe we get down to 1% to 1.5% growth, this is nothing to write home about, but it’s not recession territory.

Trading around a core position to lower my cost basis

Before I get to the heart of the matter, I did find a way to use this yo-yo volatility to our advantage. When Nasdaq jumped 12%, I trimmed off 1% to 3% of the highest-priced shares of each position. Then, when the indexes retreated, I added them back in at a lower price. l would try to get it as close to the cost basis or lower in some cases. Lowering the cost basis raises the overall profit of the investment. I still think that the market has more volatility to go through, so when stocks ended in a rally on Friday, I trimmed a bit more. If we go up straight from here, I am unafraid to hold on to the cash and wait for the next investment opportunity.

I've come to the opinion that Alphabet offers an opportunity for strong capital appreciation

I want to focus on a company and a stock that is underappreciated by market participants and most financial media. Right now, Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) is my smallest position compared to the five other big-cap names that I have been aggressively accumulating - Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Nvidia (NVDA), Boeing (BA), and Netflix (NFLX). NFLX reports this week, and if we are lucky, it will sell off hard and we can get some equity on the cheap. Putting that aside, I was inspired toward the end of this week to get bullish on GOOGL by a slew of news items that I read in none other than Seeking Alpha. I believe that GOOGL is borrowing a page from Jeff Bezos’s strategy book.

“Your margin is my opportunity,” Jeff Bezos

The first article that caught my attention was this neutral news piece that didn’t draw any conclusions, as good news reporting shouldn’t. Google in the spotlight as the tech giant signs a deal with the US govt to cut software prices. Quite simply, GOOGL is providing their office software suite of Google Mail, Docs, etc. for 71% off for the GSA as a US Government-wide contract until September. This seems inconsequential since it's a very short-term agreement. That is, if you take that fact at face value, I don’t. I see this as a gambit to take share from Microsoft Office 365. From now until September, it is my opinion that the “DOGE boys” and the GSA will measure user experience and productivity. MSFT maintains a lot of versions of Office, both server and cloud, and Google applications are and always been a cloud service, so it is much simpler to support. Also, in my opinion, Google Mail is more secure than Outlook.

If there is little difference between the two except cost, I would think that Google Office Applications will become the new standard if there is little difference. The heading for this paragraph is the famous saying by Jeff Bezos, “Your margin is my opportunity.” This means, even if GOOGL gets only 29% of what MSFT gets for the same cloud service, it is all pure profit to them. The Google Office bundle is already paid for, so the cost to them is practically nothing, except for cloud services. This is where it gets interesting; much of the value of GOOGL is growth, and a lot of attention is paid to Google Cloud Platform growth. If Google takes the whole contract, that is over 3M seats. That would be 3.6% of the 82.5 million seats Office 365 takes up. In 2024, Microsoft earned $55B for Office 365 products, which is a big margin. The federal government is the largest employer in the US. If it shows big savings without loss of productivity, that would also be a major marketing coup. Other very large organizations that use Office 365 might also want that 31% discount. Microsoft financial numbers for 2024

Google would have to gain more than a trillion in market value to be near the top of the list below. Nabbing the top of the list below will take more than taking market share from Microsoft Office, though it does help.

AAPL market Cap 2.97T

MSFT Market Cap $2.9T

NVDA Market Cap $2.7T

AMZN Market Cap $2.0T

GOOGL Market Cap $1.90T

Waymo is going to be in way more cities

We learned that Waymo is already an operating success in Austin. Again, Seeking Alpha provided me with another interesting item. Waymo has already taken up 20% of Uber Platform’s rides. In this same article, we learned that 22% of rides in San Francisco were served by Waymo, tying Lyft (LYFT) for market share. There was some anecdotal commentary that Waymo is undersupplying cars there, which means they could capture more share. There is good news there as well, Hyundai is going to be manufacturing Waymo Ionic 5s customized with Waymo equipment. Ionic testing to begin later this year, so they should grow their share in San Francisco. Still, Waymo is not sitting on its hands; they are branching out in many major cities in the USA. Waymo robotaxis also operate in Phoenix and Los Angeles. Waymo plans to expand the service to Atlanta, Miami, and Washington, D.C, and they are expanding internationally, starting in Tokyo with mapping city streets.

The projected market is huge. Coherent Market Insights projects the market to reach USD 135.74 billion by 2032, with a CAGR of 64.1% from 2025 to 2032. According to CNBC, Tesla (TSLA) projects the robotaxi market to reach $1.7T by 2040. Speaking of TSLA, they are just getting started in Austin, yet they have been valued largely on their robotaxi potential and robotics. Suffice to say that the market opportunity is huge, and Waymo is way ahead.

TSLA market cap is 1.4T, Toyota Motor (TM) market cap is $221 B. I am being very generous in valuing TSLA's car business and energy storage, and any other revenue to be equal to that of Toyota, arguably the best-run automobile company in the world. So that means that future TSLA is worth about $1.2T as of Friday. I can easily argue that GOOGL should have an equal amount of market value due to it being the leading robotaxi company, and TSLA has hardly gotten started. It should add at least $1T to GOOGL‘s valuation right there. As seen above, TSLA's current cap is $1.9T, add another $1T, and it is right in the running for king of the hill at $2.9T

The elephant in the room, Google Search is losing revenue

With all the negativity about Google Search, I bet you didn’t blink with the above heading stating that revenue was going down. Google Search and other advertising revenue increased by 12% to $54 billion in Q4 2024. I’m not saying that GOOGL’s primacy in search is not being challenged, of course it is, but this is not a difficult nut to crack. GOOGL will offer paid search (blue links), organic search results, and a generative AI solution. There are some indications that advertising growth is slowing, but it is still growing in a slowing economy. Google search is so huge that it does get affected by macro forces. Meanwhile, Google Cloud revenue is profitable and growing 30% a year.

Other growth drivers I didn’t even touch on YouTube being the largest streamer according to MarketWatch. YouTube is the dominant TV streamer and podcaster. In 2024, YouTube advertising rose 14% to $36 billion. Just recently, Alphabet made its biggest acquisition, acquiring Wiz for $35B, an advanced, fast-growing cybersecurity technology that works on multiple clouds, not just GCP. This cybersecurity offering is made for the AI era. It is hard at this point to see what contributions it could make as far as generating revenue. Perhaps the US GSA gave Google applications the opportunity it got because of the Wiz acquisition.

There is no hard and fast formula to arrive at a market cap

One overarching theme is that when a company starts initiating more growth initiatives in rapid succession, the stock gets more attention. The stock market values growth highly, and a company like Alphabet with 2 relatively new businesses, a growing and profitable cloud business, and the fastest growing robotaxi business in the US, in my opinion, should elevate GOOGL to the top of the heap. I am a buyer of GOOGL, and I could see GOOGL approach 260+ per share in 18 months if it gets the recognition it deserves, and proves that it can hold on to the Google Search. GOOGL is an excellent long-term investment with strong potential to be revalued higher once all the new initiatives are recognized. How did I arrive at 260 per share? I took the comparable valuation from TSLA and added $1T to the value of GOOGL. I am not taking anything away from TSLA, I am just saying that if TSLA gets that valuation, so does GOOGL. I added the other growth drivers like the growth of YouTube, the renewed push to take market share from MSFT Office, the very important notion that GOOGL will fix search and integrate generative AI into it. Wiz is a wild card; they have to keep it compatible with MSFT Azure and AWS, which means it is seen as a stand-alone revenue generator. Can it steal share from Palo Alto Networks (PANW) or CrowdStrike (CRWD), perhaps, perhaps not. The Cybersecurity market has a lot of players, and if each gives up a little, Wiz could make a lot.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IDEXX Laboratories: I Love The Business, Not The Stock Price
data.description:
Introduction

IDEXX's (NASDAQ:IDXX) stated vision for the company is,

To be a great company that creates exceptional long-term value for our customers, employees, and shareholders by enhancing the health and well-being of pets, people, and livestock.

The company aims to achieve this vision through six avenues, as shown below.

The Company's Revenue Drivers

IDXX reportable revenue has three segments, the primary one is CAG or Companion Animal Group, "a reporting segment that provides veterinarians diagnostic products and services and information management solutions that enhance the health and well-being of pets" (2024 10K).

Other than CAG, IDXX also reports revenue from Water quality products, and Livestock, Poultry and Dairy (LPD). The Other operating segment is very small, making up only 0.4% of IDXX's total revenue, so I will not be discussing that.

Five Reasons Why I Like The Business

One: Strong Recurring Revenue

I love a business with recurring revenue.

See the second-largest segment below, called "Consumables"?

Consumables make up 36% of IDXX's CAG sales in 2024, up from 35% in 2023, a segment that boasts the second-largest growth rate of 9.7%. Strictly speaking, consumables are not recurring revenue in the subscription-model sense of the word, i.e. customers are not contractually obligated to buy these consumables. IDXX does not include "consumables" under recurring revenue (more on that later). However, once IDXX's clients buy IDXX's analyzers, they have no choice but to purchase IDXX proprietary consumables frequently and predictably. And that is why I consider this stream of income as "recurring".

Let's illustrate this with one example, IDXX's Catalyst One Chemistry Analyzer, a clinic's on-site laboratory for dogs, cats, exotic animals, horses and livestock.

(This link brings you to the official operation manual, while this link brings you to a video explanation. The description below is a simplified, reconstructed version based on the video and the manual.)

Say a vet wants to find do a routine health check on a pet dog's kidney function. The vet will need a blood sample from the animal. Collecting the blood requires a syringe, a needle, and a lithium heparinized blood separator. Next, a chemistry CLIP (with preloaded set of catalyst slides) is needed to be inserted into the Catalyst One together with the blood. After that, a SDMA slide (Symmetric Dimethylarginine) and SDMA reagent are loaded into the Catalyst One. All the vet needs to do now is to press the start button, and 10 min to 15 min is all it takes for the Catalyst One analyzer to process the blood sample and display the results on the analyzer, and then subsequently transfer that data into the IDEXX VetLab Station, a touch screen, centralized hub that comes bundled with the Catalyst One (and other IDEXX analyzers) used for managing and organizing diagnostic data across the different IDEXX analyzers. On a monthly basis, the vet will conduct a calibration of Catalyst One, and to perform that the vet will need to procure IDXX's Catalyst SmartQC Solution.

All the consumables mentioned above (syringe, needle, lithium heparinized blood separator, SDMA slide and reagent, SmartQC Solution) are not meant to be reused, so new ones have to be purchased from IDXX for the duration of use for Catalyst One.

And IDXX sells other analyzers, not just Catalyst One, each with its set of proprietary consumables, so once a consumer buys an analyzer, the company benefits from recurring revenues from consumables.

How would the data be collected across these different analyzers? Regardless of the analyzers used, the data is consolidated across different IDXX's analyzers for each patient, making the documenting and referencing of patient records easy. This streamlined process makes it easy for the vet to collect data electronically rather than manually, reducing errors.

Then, there is the smaller segment called "recurring revenue", under Veterinary software, services and diagnostic imaging systems. This is the software part. There are two software options for customers to choose from. There is ezyVet, a cloud-based practice management software that improves team productivity and streamlines patient care. There is also Neo Software, a cloud-based software with an intuitive dashboard to help users manage daily activity, manage appointments, get a clear overview of overdue invoices, reminders due and draft invoices, has a one-click feature to access complete patient history, and much more.

Although this portion of the total revenue is not the largest, just 7% of the CAG's revenue segment, but its growth is the highest at 16.7%. Without this recurring revenue portion, CAG's total revenue growth will fall from 6.6% to 5.9%.

Over time, as the number of users expands, the software segment will also grow and will become a significant contributor to IDXX's overall revenue and profit (profit margin is the highest for software), which can pull up the overall results from any lag in sales growth of the hardware.

Two: Sticky Business Model

I love business with a sticky business model.

As described in the Catalyst One example above, it is easy to imagine that once IDXX's customers start to use the products, software, training services, build up their contacts with their clients, and consolidated the patients' medical history in one easy-to-access screen, it can be difficult, costly, and time-consuming to switch to a different service provider. It is sticky.

This is why CEO Jay Mazelsky was confident in declaring at Q4 2024 earnings call,

Our partnership approach has yielded exceptional customer retention rates of over 97%, ensuring that practices continue to see IDEXX as an indispensable partner in their growth and efficiency goals. Customers trust IDEXX to help them navigate challenges of staffing and visit trends.

IDXX's sales team works well with the customers, providing them with excellent support, and is able to sell to new customers and upsell to many existing customers. CEO Mazelsky continued,

IDEXX commercial teams continue to operate at a high-level this year, supporting new business gains, solid net price realization, high-90%s customer retention levels across major testing modalities, and sustained adoption levels of diagnostic testing utilization near post-pandemic highs. High placement levels across our core premium instrument platforms drove a 9% growth in our premium instrument installed base.

The following note from Morningstar analyst Debbie S. Wang is illuminating,

Beyond these technology-based intangibles, Idexx’s major secret weapon lies in its sales representatives and field organization that provide high-touch service to animal hospitals. Idexx reps have proven themselves successful in providing counsel on optimizing hospital operations and improving the profit profile for vets. We have yet to see any other animal health firms carve out this sort of integrated role in animal hospital operations.

Three: Strong Tailwinds

I love business that operates in an industry bolstered by strong tailwinds.

According to data from CapitalOneShopping, the US pet industry revenue has been growing steadily, more than doubled since 2016.

Pet owners are willing to spend on their pets, and the total vet-related expenditure (surgical vet + routine vet visit) comes to a huge 42%.

Four: Predictable Revenue

I love business with predictable and growing revenue.

Over the past twenty years, IDXX managed to grow revenue every year, through the Great Financial Crisis, the crash in 2018, COVID-19, and the bear market from 2022 to 2023. FactSet analysts are projecting continual growth moving forward, and with 100% accuracy rate for their 1-year and 2-year sales forecast, this is as predictable as one can get.

And revenue is not just growing in the United States but internationally as well. CEO Mazelsky shared at the Q4 2024 earnings call that,

Our international regions showcased strong performance in 2024, contributing significantly to our growth story. Double-digit expansion of our premium instrument installed base outside the US underscored the benefits of our targeted global commercial investments over the past three years. Our maturing international sales organization has not only secured high-quality placements but also driven consistent diagnostic utilization gains, particularly in the Europe and Asia-Pacific regions, where we continue to see untapped potential. Spotlighting our European region's very strong performance, we saw the seventh consecutive quarter of double-digit CAG Diagnostics recurring revenue growth, with IDEXX VetLab consumables growth in the high-teens.

Five: Innovation Led Growth Strategy

IDXX spends an increasing amount on R&D every year.

The amount spent in the last five years ($968 million) increased by 72% to the amount spent in the previous five years ($561 million). That helped the company come up with new products and grew the revenue by 59%, from $9.966 billion in 2015 to $$16.849 billion in 2024.

One example of the latest product is the IDEXX inVue Dx Cellular Analyzer that provides "automated, real-time cytology and blood morphology provide results with reference laboratory-level accuracy, within 10 minutes", a new tool that saves vets precious time. CEO Mazelsky says this tool is well received,

The launch of IDEXX inVue Dx has been one of the most exciting developments in 2024, marking a new era in Point-of-Care diagnostics...

Customer interest for IDEXX inVue Dx continues to be exceptionally strong, with nearly 1,600 preorders globally by the end of Q4, reflecting the high demand for this next-generation platform. International interest has been similarly robust as we began taking orders in select geographies in Q4...

... we initiated a controlled launch of IDEXX inVue Dx in Q4 and, to ensure a best-in-class customer experience, we will gradually ramp up placements through 2025...

Customers are simply amazed with how easy the analyzer is to use, eliminating the need for complex slide preparation or interpretation, with results in minutes.

Underpinning the financial guidance Andrew shared is a placement target for 4,500-plus inVue placements over the course of the year, as we march towards our 20,000 placement goal over five years.

Four Risks To Keep Tabs On

One: Declining Growth Rate

Although the average growth rate for the past twenty years was an impressive 13.33%, that declined to 8.03% for the past three years. The forward growth for FY 2025 is expected to be even lower, at just 6.36%.

FactSet analysts have repeatedly lowered their sales forecast for IDXX over the past 6 months for FY 2025, 2026 and 2027. CFO Brian McKeon explained the declining growth rate,

We continue to work through pressure on US same-store clinical visit levels, which declined nearly 3% in Q4 and 2% for the full year in 2024. This has been the primary constraint on IDEXX's recent growth, reflecting transitional sector macro impacts following the accelerated expansion of pet healthcare during the pandemic.

Basically, IDXX has tough comps after the blistering 14% and 19% growth rate for 2020 and 2021 respectively pulled forward a lot of sales. Another reason would be macro headwinds, such as fears of a global economic downturn or recession. The effects of tariffs increasing the cost of goods will be touched on next.

Two: China Risk

Senior VP Andrew Emerson acknowledged negative impacts from tariffs,

We're planning for solid gross margin gains on a comparable basis in 2025, supported by growth in CAG Diagnostics recurring revenues, benefits from lab productivity initiatives, and expansion of our high-margin cloud-based software business. This is partially offset by unfavorable business mix planned from higher levels of CAG Diagnostics instrument revenue gains and preliminary estimates for tariff risk on internationally-sourced materials.

The earnings call was held on February 2, 2025, so these estimates could not have factored in the chain of events that happened since then.

You can read more about these events in my previous article (link here).

The China risk is real, but it is minimal. Not only is the revenue segment from China small to begin with, just 0.92% in 2024, that small amount represents a 27% decline from the 1.46% in 2022.

What is unknown now is the cost impact of the materials sourced from China. Due to the stickiness of the business model, it is likely that IDXX can pass the increased costs to its existing customers. It will be harder to make the case for new potential customers, to convince them to switch to IDXX's analyzers in these uncertain times. It will also be harder to upsell and cross-sell to existing customers. Management warns of this in the 2024 10K,

... additional tariffs on imported goods may increase our costs to purchase products, components, and materials. We may not be able to offset these higher costs through productivity initiatives and price increases, which may materially and adversely affect our business, results of operations, and financial condition. Any price increases we may impose may lead to declines in sales volume or loss of business...

Three: Efficiency Risks

Although revenue has been growing steadily over the years, and management has shown itself astute in its acquisitions (e.g. ezyVet is already part of the product line up) without burgeoning the debt load (long-term-debt-to-capital ratio is only at 21%; gross leverage of about 0.7x at the end of 2024 which is down from 1.3x at the end of FY 2022), there are areas of concern.

I will highlight a few.

First, the cash conversion cycle has increased from 82 days in 2020 to 108 days in 2024, signalling customers have been taking longer time to pay. This slows down the conversion of cash from sales.

Secondly, the days inventory outstanding increased from 65 days in 2020 to 91 days in 2024, indicating a slowdown in sales.

Thirdly, both ROE (return on equity) and ROIC (return on invested capital) have been on the decline since 2024. The deleveraging over the years largely caused the ROE to fall. I am less concerned with this, as I feel more assured when a company is more financially sound.

I am more concerned about the decreasing ROIC figures, as it shows how effectively a company generates profit from its total invested capital, and this is something I will keep an eye on. To be clear, IDXX's ROIC is way higher than some of its peers like Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) (8.14%) and ResMed (RMD) (20.87%).

Four: Capital Allocation Risk

Management reduced shares outstanding by 13% from 93 million shares to 82 million shares between 2014 and 2024. Furthermore, management stated plans to purchase another $1.5 billion worth of shares in 2025.

Management’s continued emphasis on share buybacks may suggest a lack of opportunities to redeploy the cash internally or through M&A. While I like shareholder friendly actions like share buybacks and I agree that can be an effective way to return excess capital opportunistically when shares are undervalued.

At current valuation levels, management can consider a more conservative way to allocate capital, taking a page from Warren Buffett's playbook to build a cash reserve in short-term Treasuries, or even start a small dividend. These alternatives to share buybacks could provide management with greater long-term flexibility while signaling to shareholders their confidence.

Valuation

IDXX did well in 2024. Earnings per share increased 6% to $10.67. The company's 2025 earnings per share growth outlook is $11.74 per share to $12.24 per share, an increase of 8% to 12%.

Management's initial full-year earnings per share guidance of $11.74 - $12.24 reflects confidence for solid organic revenue growth. Again, note that the guidance was given on February 3, 2025, before Liberation Day.

FactSet analysts have been lowering their adjusted operating earnings over the past six months. Considering the effects of tariffs, it is fair to lower the projections further.

IDXX has an excellent record of besting analysts' 1-year forecast (45%) and 2-year forecast (36%), and meeting their 1-year forecast (45%) and 2-year forecast (64%), showing the reliability of IDXX's ability to generate consistent and predictable earnings.

Noting that IDXX's adjusted operating earning suffered during recessionary periods as well in the form of lower growth (6% growth in 2009), and with the increasing possibility of recession in 2025 and US GDP slowing down, I adjusted bear-case scenario growth rate down to just 3% in 2025, followed by 4% in 2026 and 2027, increased the possibility of my bear-case scenario happening to 30%. I derived at a $397.80 fair value for IDXX, ranging from $249.71 (36% downside) at my low range to $545.43 (39% upside) in my bull scenario. At the current price of $390.94, I do not see a margin of safety nor a favorable risk-reward-ratio. I believe I have been generous in my valuation as I continued to assign a high multiple (ranging from 20 to 30) for a company that is projected to grow at an average low double-digits growth rate for the next few years, deciding that the quality of the company deserves this premium even in the face of potential recession.

Conclusion

I love many things about IDEXX Laboratories. It is a business that is supported by strong tailwinds, has a sticky business model with predictable, recurring revenue, all supported by an innovation-led growth strategy. New products like IDEXX inVue Dx Cellular Analyzer will be supporting new growth both in the US as well as in international markets from 2025.

At the same time, there are some concerns that as an investor, I would want to be cognizant of, like the way management is allocating capital, the tariff risks involving China, and a declining growth rate.

My main concern will be the current valuation, and as someone who would consider an investment only when I see at least a 10% margin of safety, I have to reluctantly put this on my watchlist.

data.title: MSTY: Payouts Continue To Decline As Bitcoin Cycle Shifts
data.description:
Overview

YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:MSTY) is arguably one of the most successful high yielding option funds that has been launched in the last year. MSTY currently offers investors a massive distribution yield of 82.8%, based on the most recently paid distribution. The actual dividend yield changes every month as the payout amounts are declared but such a large yield like this provides a ton of utility for investors seeking a high level of income. Unlike most other YieldMax funds, MSTY hasn't fallen significantly below its inception price of approximately $20 per share. However, MSTY is a bit of a unique fund compared to other option ETFs and I believe there may be an elevated risk profile right now due to the fund's indirect association with Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that MSTY's price has fallen by over 48.5%. When actually including all of the distributions that were paid out to shareholders, MSTY's total return jumps up above 48.5% over the same period. MSTY's strategy aims to mimic the daily price movements of common shares of Strategy (MSTR). In order to achieve this, MSTY implements synthetic options that can replicate price movement, but the tradeoff is that there's a capped upside potential.

MSTY's price has generally followed the momentum of Bitcoin, and I am worried about the shift in the crypto cycle. While MSTY provided superior returns when BTC crossed that $100K milestone, the outlook over the next twelve months may not be as attractive. If the price of BTC comes crashing down, it's safe to assume that MSTY will follow that same momentum. So let's start by taking a look at MSTY's underlying investment strategy and compare how the fund has performed against YieldMax peers.

Fund Strategy & Performance Comparison

MSTY has an inception dating back to February 2024, making it only one-year old. Despite the high yield offered, MSTY has a very reasonable gross expense ratio of 0.99%. In order to generate the necessary income that it needs to fund the distributions, MSTY deploys a synthetic option strategy. The term 'synthetic' refers to the fact that MSTY doesn't actually own any common shares of the Strategy. This can elevate the risk profile and cause MSTY's price to experience a higher level of volatility compared to MSTR. MSTY replicates the daily price movements by simultaneously buying call options and selling MSTR put options. The prospectus lays out the different techniques that the fund managers use to generate income.

The strategy is effective because MSTR is such a high volatile stock. For instance, the implied volatility currently sits at 97%. The higher the volatility of a holding, the higher the income premium that can be collected from options. The volatility is a key piece of the equation to why MSTY can continue generating such high amounts of income for shareholders.

MSTY chooses to implement call options that are ATM (at-the-money), which involves selecting a strike price that is at or near the current market price of MSTR. This allows MSTY to generate a sizable amount of premium income from the transaction but doesn't allow for much capital appreciation to be experienced. Plenty of other YieldMax funds using this strategy have already demonstrated that this strategy will usually lead to capital erosion over time.
